title: Split, Project, and Concat
concept: Inputs are projected into Query, Key, and Value spaces per head, processed independently, and their outputs are concatenated and projected back.

exercises.prompt: How does a transformer look at multiple types of relationships in a sentence at once?

options.text: By splitting, projecting, and processing information in parallel heads.
By using only a single head and ignoring all other context.
By converting all words into random numbers.

exercises.explain: Multi-head attention lets the model look at different parts of a sequence at the same time using different focus spaces.

cards.title: Divide and Conquer
cards.body: Have you ever tried to solve a complex puzzle with a friend? You might look for corner pieces, while they look for specific colors. Multi-head attention does the exact same thing! It splits the work, lets different 'heads' look at different features, and then merges their ideas back together.

cards.title: Split, Project, and Concat
cards.body: Instead of looking at the data in one single way, multi-head attention splits the input data and projects (transforms) it into Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) spaces for each head. After each head processes its part independently, the outputs are concatenated (glued back together) and projected one final time back to the original size.

cards.title: The Multi-Tasking Assistants
cards.body: Imagine a big corporate project. Instead of one manager trying to read, summarize, and file every single document at the same time, they hire three specialist assistants. One assistant highlights what we are searching for (Queries), another tags the folders (Keys), and the third extracts the actual useful facts (Values). They do their work in separate rooms, and then glue their notes together at the end!

cards.title: Heads at Work
cards.body: If we have a sentence like 'The bank of the river', Head 1 might focus on the word 'bank' in the context of money, while Head 2 focuses on 'bank' in the context of land. Each head has its own set of Q, K, and V weights, works independently, and then their findings are concatenated into a single output vector.

cards.title: Not Just Copy-Pasting
cards.body: Many believe that multi-head attention just repeats the exact same calculations multiple times on the same data. In reality, each head projects the data into a completely different subspace, allowing them to look for entirely different patterns simultaneously!

cards.title: The Attention Pipeline
cards.body: See how the data flow splits apart for specialization, and then merges back together.

right.text: All head outputs are glued side-by-side and projected back into one unified vector.

left.text: The input is divided and projected into Q, K, and V spaces for multiple independent heads.

exercises.prompt: Why do we project inputs into separate Query, Key, and Value spaces for each attention head?

options.text: To allow different heads to focus on different aspects of the relationships in the text.
To reduce the number of calculations to zero.
To delete redundant words from the sentence.
To make sure all heads calculate the exact same numbers.

exercises.explain: Each head uses its own unique projection weights to create Query, Key, and Value vectors, allowing them to focus on different relationships.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The outputs of the individual attention heads are concatenated together before being projected back to the original size.
exercises.explain: Outputs from all attention heads are concatenated (joined together side-by-side) before the final linear projection.

exercises.prompt: Complete the description of the multi-head attention pipeline:
exercises.sentence: Individual head outputs are first [blank] and then projected back to the original size.
exercises.explain: After the individual attention heads finish processing their inputs in parallel, their outputs are concatenated (joined end-to-end) and projected back to the original size.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the multi-head attention workflow?

options.text: Split inputs -> Project to Q, K, V per head -> Process in parallel -> Concat outputs -> Final projection.
Project all inputs together -> Average them -> Randomly delete half the heads.
Process Head 1 -> Use Head 1's output to start Head 2 -> Process sequentially.

exercises.explain: Multi-head attention splits, projects, and processes in parallel, then concatenates. It does not average the heads' intermediate states together or run them sequentially.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a high-performance transformer layer. How does the 'Split, Project, and Concat' pipeline handle data flow?

options.text: It averages the Q, K, and V vectors from all heads before calculating any attention.
It projects inputs into Q, K, V for each head, runs attention in parallel, joins the results, and projects them back.
It discards all values except for the single head that gets the highest score.
It forces every head to look at the exact same information without any projection.

exercises.explain: Multi-head attention splits the incoming vectors, projects them into Q, K, and V spaces for each head, processes them in parallel, and then concatenates the results before passing them through a final output projection layer.
